Kyle J. Card, Ph.D.
CVPublicationsBlogContact

Publications

Peer-reviewed work on the predictability and control of antibiotic resistance — from historical contingency to collateral sensitivity, and the tools to measure it.

577 citations· h-index 7· Google Scholar →

Google Scholar, as of June 29, 2026

01 Selected work

⤢

Historical contingency

Historical contingency in the evolution of antibiotic resistance after decades of relaxed selection.

Card K.J., LaBar T., Gomez J.B., Lenski R.E.

PLoS Biology 17, e3000397 (2019).

After decades of evolving without antibiotics, replicate E. coli lines differed in how readily they could evolve resistance — early evidence that a population's deep history constrains its future adaptability.

DOI →

⤢

Fitness costs

Idiosyncratic variation in the fitness costs of tetracycline-resistance mutations in Escherichia coli.

Card K.J., Jordan J.A., Lenski R.E.

Evolution 75, 1230–1238 (2021).

The fitness cost of a tetracycline-resistance mutation depended idiosyncratically on the genome it arose in — a complication for strategies that assume resistance always carries a predictable price.

DOI →

⤢

Genetic background

Genomic evolution of antibiotic resistance is contingent on genetic background following a long-term experiment with Escherichia coli.

Card K.J., Thomas M., Graves J.L., Barrick J.E., Lenski R.E.

Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 118, e2016886118 (2021).

Identical selection drove different E. coli backgrounds down different mutational routes to resistance — showing that genetic history shapes not just whether resistance evolves, but which genes carry it.

DOI →

⤢

Open tools

Science Forum: A low-cost, open-source evolutionary bioreactor and its educational use.

Gopalakrishnan V.*, Crozier D.*, Card K.J.*, Krishnan N.P., McClure E., Pelesko J., Williamson D.F.K., Webster N., Nichol D., Mandal S., Bonomo R.A., Scott J.G.

eLife 11, e83067 (2022).

A low-cost, open-source bioreactor that makes continuous experimental evolution affordable and reproducible — the kind of accessible instrument the Evolution-on-a-Chip program is built on.

DOI →

⤢

Collateral sensitivity

Evolution under vancomycin selection drives divergent collateral sensitivity patterns in Staphylococcus aureus.

Card K.J.*, Crozier D.*, Durmaz A., Gray J., Creary J., Stocks A., Maltas J., Bonomo R.A., Burke Z.D.C., Scott J.G.

Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 122, e2507962122 (2025).

Staphylococcus aureus evolved vancomycin resistance by at least two mutually exclusive genetic routes — each leaving a distinct, partly predictable pattern of collateral sensitivity to second-line drugs. This is the work behind the Collateral Response Score.

DOI →
